claim: Outcome: LOSS — the client chose the competitor LogiCore, contract signed for 2 years.
quote: he flat out said: I'm for LogiCore, and that was that, after that we were just finalizing paperwork

claim: Confirmation of the outcome — the contract with the competitor is already signed for a 2-year term.
quote: the contract with LogiCore is for two years

claim: List of alternatives: Stackly (us), LogiCore, a third local vendor filtered out on price; the option of "not changing anything" was also discussed.
quote: We looked at three options. You, Stackly

claim: The third vendor was filtered out at an early stage due to price.
quote: that fell through right away on price, they turned out too expensive for us

claim: The option of "keeping the old system / doing nothing" was discussed but rejected after incidents of lost shipments.
quote: after we lost a shipment twice in April because of confusion in the stock counts, everyone agreed we couldn't keep dragging it out

claim: Comparison criteria: price, functionality for product labeling (mandatory labeling requirement), quality of support, reputation/recommendations.
quote: Well, price, of course, can't get around that

claim: Functionality criterion — product labeling under the mandatory labeling requirement.
quote: Functionality for product labeling — that's critical for us, the mandatory labeling requirement and all that

claim: Support criterion — driven by a past negative experience of slow tech support.
quote: we'd already gotten burned when tech support didn't answer for three days straight on the previous system

claim: Reputation criterion — vendor recommendations and industry case studies.
quote: reputation — who these vendors even are, who recommended them, whether there are case studies in our industry

claim: Decisive moment — LogiCore's second demo, where the architect spun up a live test environment in real time and showed the full cycle of goods receiving with labeling in front of the logistics director.
quote: Their architect, right in front of our logistics director, spun up a live test environment in real time and showed goods receiving with labeling, the whole cycle, in about ten minutes

claim: At the moment of the demo, the logistics director openly signaled the decision was leaning toward LogiCore.
quote: look, this is exactly what we need, no song and dance

claim: Formal decision belonged to the procurement director (the respondent), but the decisive opinion turned out to be the logistics director's, as the primary future user of the system.
quote: Formally the decision is mine, I'm the procurement director

claim: The logistics director carried the most weight in the decision, since he would be working with the system every day.
quote: everyone listened hard to the logistics director, he's the one who'll be working with the system every day, his word carried a lot of weight

claim: The CFO mostly signed off on the numbers rather than deciding; the CEO delegated the choice, asking only about vendor reliability.
quote: The CEO delegated it pretty much entirely, asked only once — is the vendor reliable at all, won't they go under in a year

claim: We (Stackly) responded faster — the next day, demo 4 days later; LogiCore responded slower (about 3 days), but arrived with a scenario tailored to the client's profile.
quote: You responded fast, literally the day after we submitted the request, that's a plus

claim: LogiCore's demo was precisely tailored to the client's specifics (distribution, three warehouses, labeling), not a generic template.
quote: it was straight away with a ready-made scenario built for our profile, for distribution, not a generic template

claim: On our first call, the manager asked general questions, as if unfamiliar with the client's specifics.
quote: your manager on the first call was asking general stuff, like starting from zero, we had to explain everything all over again

claim: At our demo, the engineer couldn't immediately answer about 1C integration; at LogiCore's demo, an architect was present and answered thoroughly.
quote: On your demo, the engineer straight up couldn't answer a question about 1C integration on the spot, said he'd check and get back

claim: Price: we were about 15% cheaper; LogiCore was 12-15% more expensive, the client knowingly overpaid for ready-made labeling functionality to avoid downtime and audit risk.
quote: On price you were about fifteen percent cheaper, that was nice, not gonna lie

claim: LogiCore's price was 12-15% higher, but the client agreed to it knowingly.
quote: LogiCore's price, by the way, was higher than yours by about twelve to fifteen percent, but we agreed to it

claim: The client was willing to overpay specifically for the absence of custom dev work and its associated downtime/audit risks.
quote: we overpaid for the functionality already being ready and not having to wait for custom dev, for us that was worth more than the money saved

claim: KEY DIFFERENTIATOR — THE COMPETITOR'S READY-MADE LABELING FUNCTIONALITY, DEMONSTRATED VIA A LIVE ENVIRONMENT, PLUS THE LOGISTICS DIRECTOR'S PERSONAL TRUST THROUGH A CONTACT FROM A PREVIOUS COMPANY (unstable cell — the client himself splits the official and unofficial versions).
quote: That trust through someone he knew, I think, really tipped the scales, and the labeling — that's more like a nice, presentable justification for the board

claim: REASON CATEGORY — RELATIONSHIP-TRUST (the real motive) UNDER THE OFFICIAL VERSION OF PRODUCT (the stated reason for the board).
quote: in reality, probably around sixty percent of it was personal trust plus that impressive demo with the live environment

claim: WEAK SPOT IN THE SALES PROCESS — FIRST CALL NOT TAILORED TO THE CLIENT'S PROFILE AND NO TECHNICAL EXPERT AT THE DEMO ABLE TO ANSWER ABOUT INTEGRATIONS ON THE SPOT.
quote: make sure there's a technical person right there at the demo who can answer about integrations, instead of pushing it to later

claim: REVERSIBILITY OF THE OUTCOME — THERE'S A CHANCE IN A YEAR TO A YEAR AND A HALF IF READY-MADE LABELING APPEARS AND THE COMPETITOR STUMBLES ON IMPLEMENTATION; THE CONTRACT WITH THE COMPETITOR IS ALREADY SIGNED FOR 2 YEARS.
quote: I wouldn't rule out that in a year or a year and a half we'd take another look

claim: CREDIBILITY OF THE EXPLANATION — THE STATED REASON (READY-MADE FUNCTIONALITY) IS PARTLY A POLITE ONE: THE CLIENT HIMSELF DIRECTLY DISTINGUISHES THE OFFICIAL VERSION FOR THE RECORD FROM THE REAL MOTIVE OF PERSONAL ACQUAINTANCE (unstable cell — the contribution share of each factor is eyeballed by the client, "around sixty percent").
quote: off the record, honestly, I feel like it wasn't only about the labeling

claim: The client himself confirms the gap between the official and real reason with a confirming word.
quote: There's something to that, yeah

claim: KEY INSIGHT: the LOSS outcome was determined by the factor of ready-made labeling functionality, demonstrated via a live environment, reinforced by the logistics director's personal trust in LogiCore through a former colleague, at the moment of the second demo in the third week of the process — for sales, this means a demo for the distribution segment needs a live environment with labeling and a technical expert from the first call, not a talk about architecture.
quote: In your position I'd show a live environment with labeling right away at the demo for distribution clients, not slides with a roadmap
